era_context: The Old Kingdom of Egypt is in its final phase (Dynasty VI, c. 2278–2184 BCE). The Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin dominates Mesopotamia and the Levant. In the Indus Valley, the mature Harappan civilization is at its height. China's Xia Dynasty (traditional dating) is in its legendary early period; archaeological evidence for this era remains contested. This is a period of centralized state power, long-distance trade networks, and monumental architecture across multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (no written records until much later)
Eastern Europe and the Steppe (sparse archaeological evidence)

events.title: Reign of Pepi II of Egypt (Dynasty VI) — continued rule
events.description: Pepi II (Neferkare) continues his exceptionally long reign, traditionally dated to 64 years (c. 2278–2184 BCE), though modern scholars debate the actual length. His reign is marked by continued monumental building, trade expeditions to Nubia and the Levant, and the gradual decentralization of power to provincial governors (nomarchs). By this period, the central authority of the Old Kingdom is weakening, a trend that will accelerate toward the First Intermediate Period.

sources.name: Manetho's Aegyptiaca (preserved in later Greek sources)
Autobiographical inscriptions from provincial tombs (e.g., Harkhuf, Sabni)

events.certainty_note: The reign of Pepi II is well-attested in Egyptian sources, but the traditional length of 64 years is disputed by modern Egyptologists. Dating to specific years within his reign is approximate. The year -2279 falls within his reign under most chronologies, but the exact regnal year is uncertain.

events.title: Naram-Sin of Akkad — consolidation of empire
events.description: Naram-Sin (r. c. 2254–2218 BCE) continues military campaigns to expand and consolidate Akkadian control over Mesopotamia, the Levant, and parts of Anatolia. He is the first Akkadian ruler to declare himself divine (deification of the king), a radical innovation. His reign is marked by military victories, administrative reorganization, and the spread of Akkadian language and culture. However, internal rebellions and external pressure from the Gutians are beginning to destabilize the empire.

sources.name: Akkadian royal inscriptions (Victory Stele of Naram-Sin, etc.)
Cuneiform administrative tablets from Akkadian period

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-documented in contemporary inscriptions and administrative records. However, absolute dating relies on the Mesopotamian chronology, which has multiple competing systems (short, middle, long). The year -2279 falls within his reign under most chronologies, but the exact regnal year is uncertain by ±10–20 years.

events.title: Harappan civilization — mature phase urban centers
events.region: Indus Valley (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization (Harappan culture) is at its mature phase, with major urban centers at Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, Dholavira, and Kalibangan. These cities are characterized by planned street grids, standardized weights and measures, sophisticated drainage systems, and undeciphered script. Long-distance trade networks connect the Indus Valley to Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia. The civilization's exact political organization remains unknown, but evidence suggests either a decentralized network of city-states or a loosely unified state.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, and other sites
Indus script inscriptions on seals and pottery
Mesopotamian cuneiform references to 'Meluhha' (likely the Indus Valley)

events.certainty_note: The existence and characteristics of the Harappan civilization in this period are confirmed by extensive archaeological evidence. However, the exact chronology of the mature phase is debated; most scholars place it c. 2600–1900 BCE, which includes -2279. The year -2279 is assigned to this period by convention, but the civilization's internal chronology is not year-specific.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional length of Pepi II's reign (64 years) is increasingly questioned by modern Egyptologists, who suggest it may have been significantly shorter (20–30 years). This affects the absolute dating of events within his reign. The Akkadian chronology is contested: the 'long chronology' places Naram-Sin's reign c. 2254–2218 BCE, while the 'short chronology' places it c. 2190–2154 BCE, a difference of ~60 years. The Indus script remains undeciphered, so claims about Harappan political organization or specific events are speculative. No contemporary written records from the Indus Valley civilization exist in a deciphered language.
historiographic_note: This period is documented through Egyptian administrative and tomb inscriptions (biased toward elite activities), Akkadian royal inscriptions (propagandistic), and Mesopotamian administrative tablets (fragmentary). The Indus Valley is known almost entirely through archaeology, with no deciphered written records. The historical record is heavily skewed toward Mesopotamia and Egypt; other regions (Sub-Saharan Africa, Europe, East Asia) are virtually invisible in written sources. The dating of all three civilizations depends on competing chronological systems, introducing uncertainty of ±10–50 years. Oral traditions and later compilations (Manetho, Sumerian King List) are the only sources for some events, introducing legendary elements.

graph_edges.note: Pepi II's long reign and decentralization of power contributed to the collapse of the Old Kingdom
Naram-Sin's reign continues until c. 2218 BCE
The mature Harappan civilization eventually declined around 1900 BCE
